name: Monterey Bay National Marine Sanctuary
description: Known as the 'Serengeti of the Sea', featuring a massive underwater canyon that brings cold, nutrient-rich water to the surface, supporting kelp forests and giant mammals.

speciesPool.name: Blue Whale
speciesPool.lore: The largest animal known to have ever lived, reaching weights of up to 200 tons. Their heart is the size of a small car, and their low-frequency calls can travel hundreds of miles across oceans.

speciesPool.name: Humpback Whale
speciesPool.lore: Famous for their majestic breaches and complex, haunting songs. In Monterey Bay, they perform cooperatively to create spiral 'bubble nets' that trap and herd schools of small fish.

speciesPool.name: Great White Shark
speciesPool.lore: One of the ocean's most formidable predators. They aggregate around the Monterey Bay area in autumn (referred to as the 'Red Triangle') to feed on rich seal populations.

speciesPool.name: Southern Sea Otter
speciesPool.lore: An essential keystone species of Monterey Bay. By consuming sea urchins, they protect kelp forests from being completely deforested, preserving critical nursery habitat for countless species.

speciesPool.name: Leopard Shark
speciesPool.lore: Stunning spotted sharks that frequent shallow sandy bays and eelgrass beds. They pose absolutely no danger to humans and are active nocturnal hunters.

name: Great Barrier Reef Marine Park
description: The world's largest coral reef ecosystem, visible from space, composed of over 2,900 individual reefs and supporting an astonishing diversity of colorful marine life.

speciesPool.name: Tiger Shark
speciesPool.lore: Identified by dark tiger-like stripes. They are solitary, night-active hunters and crucial for maintaining the health of turtle populations and seagrass beds on the reef.

speciesPool.name: Green Sea Turtle
speciesPool.lore: Strictly herbivorous as adults, grazing on seagrasses which helps keep the beds productive. They migrate thousands of miles across open seas to nest on the same beaches where they hatched.

speciesPool.name: Indo-Pacific Bottlenose Dolphin
speciesPool.lore: Highly social and incredibly intelligent. They navigate the complex coral lagoons using vocal clicks and whistle whistles, cooperating in groups to herd school fish.

speciesPool.name: Reef Manta Ray
speciesPool.lore: Boasting the largest brain-to-body ratio of any fish. They glide gracefully above coral outcrops to visit 'cleaning stations' where cleaner wrasses feed on their parasites.

speciesPool.name: Whale Shark
speciesPool.lore: The largest fish in the world. Their skin is decorated with a unique checkerboard pattern of spots, which scientists use like fingerprints to identify individuals.

name: Galápagos Marine Reserve
description: A unique confluence of major warm and cold ocean currents, fostering a melting pot of evolutionary biodiversity and species found nowhere else on earth.

speciesPool.name: Scalloped Hammerhead Shark
speciesPool.lore: Known for aggregating in massive schools of hundreds around Darwin and Wolf islands. Their hammer-shaped head acts like a metal detector, loaded with sensors to find buried prey.

speciesPool.name: Galápagos Marine Iguana
speciesPool.lore: The only lizard in the world that dives in the ocean to feed. They swim with a snake-like motion and dive down to graze on red and green algae growing on volcanic rocks.

speciesPool.name: Killer Whale (Orca)
speciesPool.lore: The ocean's ultimate apex predator. Operating in tight matrilineal pods, they hunt sea lions and rays in the Galápagos waters using highly coordinated tactics.

speciesPool.name: Galápagos Penguin
speciesPool.lore: The only penguin species that lives north of the equator. They survive in the tropical zone thanks to the chilly, nutrient-rich Humboldt and Cromwell currents.
